title: The Architecture of the Psyche
subtitle: Emotional intelligence, thinking, and inner resilience
description: The deepest level of working with reality is working with your own mind. This checklist combines key concepts from cognitive and behavioral psychology, neuroscience, and mindfulness practices for building a resilient and flexible psyche.

items.title: Neuroplasticity
items.source: Norman Doidge — The Brain That Changes Itself
items.text: The brain is not a fixed structure, but a constantly changing organ. 'Neurons that fire together wire together.' Thoughts and actions we repeat literally carve pathways in the brain, making certain reactions automatic. The good news: you can lay down new pathways.
items.tip: Every time you react differently in an old situation, you are changing the physical structure of your brain.

items.title: Cognitive Fusion and Defusion
items.text: We suffer not from thoughts, but from believing thoughts are facts. 'I'm a failure' is just an electromagnetic wave in the cortex, not a reflection of reality. Defusion is the ability to observe thoughts from the outside, like clouds in the sky.
items.tip: Add the phrase 'I'm having the thought that...' to a scary thought (e.g., 'I'm having the thought that I'll fail the presentation').

items.title: Karpman's Triangle
items.text: In conflicts and stress, people unconsciously take one of three roles: Victim, Persecutor, or Rescuer. All three roles are destructive and deprive one of an adult stance. The way out is to take the position of Observer or Author of your own life.
items.tip: If you feel being 'rescued' or being a 'victim', ask: 'What action can I take right now as an adult?'

items.title: Acceptance and Control
items.text: Suffering arises from trying to control the uncontrollable (other people's thoughts, weather, the past). Maturity is a clear division: 'What depends on me?' and 'What does not depend on me?' All energy is directed only into the first zone.
items.tip: Draw a circle. Inside, write everything you can influence. Outside — everything else. Focus on the inside.

items.title: Mindfulness Without Meditation
items.text: Mindfulness is not necessarily sitting in lotus pose. It is simply actively noticing the new in the familiar. When you walk a familiar route and actually look at the color of the walls or the shape of the clouds — that's a practice that brings you back to the 'here and now'.
items.tip: Choose a routine action (brushing teeth) and perform it with your non-dominant hand. Your brain will wake up.

items.title: Post-Traumatic Growth
items.text: Trauma destroys the old worldview, but on these ruins often grows a stronger and more meaningful value system. People who have experienced hardship report greater appreciation of life and deeper relationships. Pain is inevitable, suffering is optional.
items.tip: In difficult situations, look for the answer to: 'What is this situation trying to teach me?' (even if the question seems cynical).

items.title: Nonviolent Communication
items.text: Behind every 'You make me sick' is an unexpressed need. 'I'm angry because I need respect for my time.' Shifting focus from judging the other ('You're selfish') to your own feelings and needs dissolves conflict.
items.tip: NVC formula: 'When I see/hear [FACT], I feel [EMOTION], because I need [NEED]. Would you be willing to [REQUEST]?'

items.title: Antifragility of the Psyche
items.text: Fragile breaks under stress. Resilient returns to original. Antifragile — becomes stronger due to stress. Avoiding all difficulties makes the psyche weak. Controlled challenges (cold shower, sports, difficult conversations) strengthen it.
items.tip: Once a week, do something that causes mild discomfort but is beneficial in the long run.

items.title: Working with the Inner Critic
items.text: The voice that scolds us for mistakes evolutionarily intended to protect us from being cast out of the tribe. But now it often overdoes it. Treat yourself as you would a best friend who made a mistake. Support motivates better than criticism.
items.tip: When you hear the inner critic, mentally address yourself by name and say: '[Name], I know you're having a hard time right now. You'll get through this.'

items.title: Expressive Writing
items.text: Voicing or writing down traumatic experience structures the chaos of emotions in the limbic system and transfers it to the prefrontal cortex (logic zone). People who keep a journal of experiences have improved immunity and reduced anxiety.
items.tip: Set aside 15 minutes and write nonstop about what worries you most. Don't think about style or grammar. Then destroy the paper.

quiz.q: What is 'cognitive defusion' in ACT therapy?
a: The ability to see thoughts separately from oneself

quiz.q: What roles are part of Karpman's Triangle?
How does antifragility differ from resilience?
What effect does Pennebaker's 'expressive writing' have?
What is the main principle of Nonviolent Communication?
a: Separate facts from judgments and speak about needs
